data.title: Chevron ignored a classic bearish call: Here's why stock is rallying
data.description:
Chevron (CVX) shares have been one of the stronger performers in the energy sector, extending their recent rally with gains of more than 4% over the past week to trade around $194.65.

The stock has climbed 13.53% over the last month and is now up 28.53% year to date, easily beating the S&P 500 (SP500)'s 8.22% return during the same period.

The rally gathered pace after Chevron climbed from about $183 at the start of the week to an intraday high near $197 before settling down. While some investors locked in early profits, the stock continued to hold above its recent breakout level near $193.

One surprising part of the rally is that the stock has stayed strong even though some technical indicators have cooled. The RSI has fallen to around 60 from above 70, and the MACD has turned negative, which usually points to weaker short-term momentum.

However, instead of falling sharply, the stock has mostly moved sideways. This suggests the stock is taking a healthy pause before its next move rather than starting a bigger decline.

Investor confidence has also been helped by Piper Sandler, which recently said Chevron still looks undervalued after two major issues were resolved.

At the same time, Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings give the stock a Strong Buy rating with a 4.80 score, while Wall Street analysts continue to rate Chevron as a Buy.

data.title: Airbnb shares climb after six consecutive sessions of losses
data.description:
Airbnb (ABNB) shares snapped six straight sessions of losses on Friday, as the stock was 3% higher at $141.65.

The American company lost 7% in the preceding six sessions. So far this year, the stock has added about 3%, compared to the 8% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index (SP500).

ABNB is down 3% over the past one month.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, ABNB has a Hold rating with a score of 3.2 out of 5. The company received A+ in the prospect of profitability, while it received D- in the valuation factor.

Seeking Alpha analysts are cautious and see the stock as a Hold.

Turning to the Wall Street, 23 analysts have given the stock a Buy or above rating. 19 analysts gave the stock hold recommendation, while two gave Sell rating to ABNB.

 
 

“Airbnb is fairly resilient to any city-specific regulations, since no city represents more than 1% of its revenue, but it could become a major headache if very restrictive policies spread like wildfire to more and more cities over time,” pointed out a recent Seeking Alpha analysis.

data.title: Primis outlines $7m pretax core conversion lift for 2027, with $0.22 per share impact
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Primis Financial Corp. (FRST) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "We are very pleased with our second quarter results and pretty excited about how things are moving going into the last half of '26." (President, CEO & Director Dennis Zember)

  • "For the second quarter, we're reporting net earnings of $9.4 million or $0.38 per share." (President, CEO & Director Zember) "During the current quarter, we did book a gain on the sale of an investment in an insurance agency of about $5.9 million, and we fully offset that with a legal settlement and a reserve build on our largest office CRE." (President, CEO & Director Zember)

  • "These results include a net interest margin of about 3.45%, up a couple of basis points over last quarter." (President, CEO & Director Zember) "For the quarter, we averaged about $3.9 billion of earning assets." (President, CEO & Director Zember) "The increase in margins and earning assets, combined with really strong performance from our mortgage company allowed us to have our first quarter ever with more than $50 million of core revenue." (President, CEO & Director Zember)

  • "Collectively, classified assets declined by about $53 million or 36%" and "we built additional reserves on our largest office loan by about $5.3 million in the quarter." (President, CEO & Director Zember)

  • "Altogether, we believe the impact on next year's results is about $7 million pretax" and "It's about $0.22 per diluted share." (President, CEO & Director Zember) "I firmly believe that this announcement all but guarantees another 1.5 years of outsized operating leverage." (President, CEO & Director Zember)

  • "Primis reported earnings of $9.4 million or diluted earnings per share of $0.38 in the second quarter compared to $7.3 million or $0.30 per share in the first quarter of '26." (Executive VP & CFO Matthew Switzer)

Outlook

  • "We think where we are right now, plus or minus a basis point or 2 is probably where we'll be for the foreseeable future." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer) "We have some subordinated debt that's available to refinance that we think we're going to be able to do at some point in the next quarter or 2 and will save us probably between 200 and 250 basis points on the cost of that debt." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

  • "We expect the noninterest expense burn, excluding mortgage and Panacea to return to the $22 million to $22.5 million range in the third quarter of this year." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

  • "We have identified $6.1 million of expected earnings improvements from fully converting the core bank in all divisions onto our real-time fully digital core." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer) "This also does not include the amortization expense related to capitalized platform development costs of $0.8 million per quarter that will end in the third quarter of '27." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

  • "I don't think it will be as tremendous as what you've seen for the first half of the year." (President, CEO & Director Zember) "I think all 3 together, I think the back half of the year probably will look a little bit like the first half of the year." (President, CEO & Director Zember)

Financial Results

  • "Return on average assets was 90 basis points versus 76 basis points in the first quarter and 26 basis points a year ago" and "pretax pre-provision operating net income" was "$11.7 million." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

  • "Net interest income was approximately $33.8 million" and "Our net interest margin in the second quarter was 3.45%, up from 3.43% last quarter and 2.86% in the year ago period." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

  • "Noninterest income was $22 million in the quarter versus $13.6 million in the first quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer) "Mortgage-related noninterest income grew 44% year-over-year to $11.4 million" and "Primis mortgage closed volume was $421 million." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

  • "Our provision this quarter was $5.5 million" and "Approximately $5.3 million of the second quarter provision was related to specific reserve additions for one nonaccrual credit." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

  • "Nonperforming assets, excluding portions guaranteed by the SBA, improved to 1.45% of total assets at quarter end from 2.35% at March 31." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer) "Core net charge-offs were 53 basis points" and were "driven by one nonaccrual loan that was resolved in the quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

Q&A

  • Woody Lay, KBW: "I'd love to just get your thoughts on how you see the NIM outlook from here." Executive VP & CFO Switzer: "Plus or minus a basis point or 2 is probably where we'll be for the foreseeable future" and the company expects a sub-debt refinancing that "will save us probably between 200 and 250 basis points on the cost of that debt."

  • Lay, KBW: "Could you just remind us what the total reserve -- total specific reserve you have against that credit is now?" Executive VP & CFO Switzer: "It's a little over $11 million of reserve" and "The borrower is making payments. So it's in nonaccrual, but not 90 days past due."

  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: "How you're thinking about loan growth in the back half of the year" and "asset class perspective." President, CEO & Director Zember: "I don't think it will be as tremendous as what you've seen for the first half of the year" and "the back half of the year probably will look a little bit like the first half of the year."

  • Gunther, Stephens: "How are you guys kind of defending against" deposit cost pressure? Executive VP & CFO Switzer: "We're not -- I'm not saying we're immune to pressures on deposit costs, but arguably, we have a few more levers that we can pull." President, CEO & Director Zember: "I think our digital advantage, our national advantage just continues to pay dividends."

  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: "Did you get a new appraisal to drive some of this provision?" Executive VP & CFO Switzer: "The driver of the provision" was that "We've gone 12 months since we put this thing on nonaccrual and vacancies only moved a little bit at the margin." President, CEO & Director Zember: "We're accounting for this on a DCF versus the appraisal" and "Matt just got more aggressive with the DCF and with some assumptions."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative, centered on competition and risk containment, reflected in questions on margin durability ("magnitude of competition"), the office CRE nonperformer ("total specific reserve"), and deposit cost pressure. (Woody Lay, KBW; Russell Gunther, Stephens; Stephen Moss, Raymond James)

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, emphasizing stability and levers, including "plus or minus a basis point or 2" for NIM and a sub-debt refinance expected to "more than offset" incremental pressures, while also repeating reserve-building intent for the office credit. (Executive VP & CFO Switzer; President, CEO & Director Zember)

  • Versus last quarter, management’s margin language shifted from expectation of "a little bit more margin expansion" (Q1) to largely flat NIM "for the foreseeable future" (Q2), while maintaining confidence around operating leverage and core conversion-driven improvements. (Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q2 emphasized earnings normalization around "the recurring level" of ROA after offsetting one-time items, while Q1 emphasized operating adjustments (excluding a tax item and prior-period gains) and a clearer expectation for margin expansion through 2026. (President, CEO & Director Zember; Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

  • Q2 introduced quantified core conversion uplift and timing: "about $7 million pretax" next year (CEO) and "$6.1 million" of expected improvements "largely...in place in early 2027" plus amortization ending in "the third quarter of '27" (CFO). Q1 discussed AI as a broad catalyst without a dollar figure, and core conversion as strategic positioning. (President, CEO & Director Zember; Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

  • Analyst focus in Q2 concentrated more heavily on the office CRE reserve level and persistence (12 months on nonaccrual, DCF assumptions) and on whether NIM can hold under competitive conditions, while Q1 questions leaned more toward mortgage production outlook and overall ROA glide path. (KBW; Raymond James; Stephens)

Risks and Concerns

  • "We are seeing some pressure on the earning asset side" and "certainly some pressures in the loan pricing." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer) Management cited a mitigation lever: refinancing subordinated debt to save "between 200 and 250 basis points" on that funding cost. (Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

  • "Approximately $5.3 million of the second quarter provision was related to specific reserve additions for one nonaccrual credit" and the office borrower has shown limited vacancy improvement over 12 months. (Executive VP & CFO Switzer) Management stated, "we want to keep adding reserves there whenever we can to reduce whatever kind of earnings volatility might come out of that." (President, CEO & Director Zember)

  • "Core net charge-offs were 53 basis points" and were "driven by one nonaccrual loan that was resolved in the quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Switzer)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as a period of improved operating stability, with net interest margin held near 3.45% and a view that NIM can remain roughly flat near current levels, supported by a potential subordinated debt refinance. Leadership highlighted a large office CRE exposure as the key credit overhang, citing an "over $11 million" specific reserve and continued reserve-building. The most material new forward-looking item was quantified core conversion-related earnings improvement, with management stating next year’s impact is "about $7 million pretax" ("about $0.22 per diluted share") and outlining an early-2027 run-rate benefit alongside AI-driven productivity efforts.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Byline signals $100M-$102M Q3 2026 net interest income amid higher-for-longer rates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Byline Bancorp (BY) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Our second quarter results were excellent, a record net income." (Executive Chairman of the Board & CEO Roberto Herencia) He also said Byline’s objective "remains clear: to become the preeminent commercial bank in Chicago," and framed growth as "first and foremost, organically, and it will be disciplined," while noting Byline "will likely be a much larger organization in the next 3 to 5 years." (Executive Chairman of the Board & CEO Herencia)
  • Management reported record profitability and balance sheet growth, including net income of $40.2M and $0.90 per diluted share, with deposits of $7.9B and loans of $7.6B. (President & Director Alberto Paracchini)
  • "Given the rate outlook and our balance sheet forecast, we expect net interest income range of $100 million to $102 million for the third quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Thomas J. Bell)

Outlook

  • Management maintained its full-year loan growth framework, with "full year loan growth in the mid-single digits" and noted the key swing factor is payoff timing rather than origination levels. (Executive VP & CFO Bell; President & Director Paracchini)
  • For Q3, management guided to net interest income of $100M-$102M and said noninterest income is expected to be $14M-$15M, while gain-on-sale revenue is expected to "average $5.5 million per quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Bell)
  • Expense guidance was reiterated: "our noninterest expense full year guidance remains unchanged at $59 million to $60 million per quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Bell)

Financial Results

  • Profitability improved versus Q1 on figures management cited, including net income of $40.2M (vs. $37.6M in Q1) and diluted EPS of $0.90 (vs. $0.83). (President & Director Paracchini)
  • Revenue was reported at $118M, with net interest income of $101M and net interest margin of 4.28%; management attributed the margin decline to "higher funding costs related to a maturing balance sheet hedge and changes in earning asset mix." (President & Director Paracchini; Executive VP & CFO Bell)
  • Noninterest income rose to $17M, which management tied to "favorable fair market value marks, higher gain on sale revenue and stronger swap fee income," and wealth management "surpassed the $1 billion in assets under administration." (Executive VP & CFO Bell)
  • Credit costs were $7.2M, including net charge-offs of $4.4M and a reserve build of $2.8M; management cited criticized loans at 3.9% and nonperforming loans at $69.1M. (President & Director Paracchini; Executive VP & CFO Bell)
  • Capital actions and returns were highlighted: about 275,000 shares repurchased for $9.1M, and "our Board approved a 16.7% increase in our quarterly dividend to $0.14 per share." (President & Director Paracchini)

Q&A

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Asked about visibility into payoffs and the implied back-half loan-growth ramp; President & Director Paracchini said payoff timing is "usually the most uncertain" and added, "We think we're past the bulk of it," while reiterating confidence in underlying originations.
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Asked why expense guidance implies a second-half step-up; Executive VP & CFO Bell pointed to "employee expenses, health care costs" and "higher commissions," and President & Director Paracchini added potential hiring opportunities would be called out if team adds were material.
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Asked about M&A and buybacks; President & Director Paracchini described M&A "chatter" as "constructive" and said if no deal is executed, "we would be looking to the buyback program to return capital back to shareholders."
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde: Asked about criticized-loan cleanup and loss avoidance; Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Mark Fucinato cited upgrades after sustained improvement, a payoff from an asset sale, and "a bit of a recovery" on a prior charge-off.
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde: Asked who is pushing deposit competition; President & Director Paracchini pointed to larger institutions returning to certain CRE categories, and Executive VP & CFO Bell said commercial deposits are competitive but "not exception pricing," while CDs reflect market expectations shifting toward potential hikes.
  • Brian Martin, Brean: Asked whether charge-offs trend lower as SBA becomes smaller; President & Director Paracchini said near-term expectations remain "30 to 40 basis points" and agreed the longer-term mix shift could push charge-offs lower.
  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked on $10B timing; President & Director Paracchini said Byline is not constraining the business, but late-year balance-sheet management could be used if it meaningfully delays Durbin timing.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: Slightly negative to pressing on payoff volatility, expense step-up, deposit competition, and $10B/Durbin timing, with repeated attempts to pin down trajectory and drivers.
  • Management: Slightly positive in prepared remarks and neutral-to-confident in Q&A, emphasizing discipline and flexibility; examples included "We think we're past the bulk of it" on payoffs and reiterations that the bank is "disciplined rather than chase volume." (President & Director Paracchini)
  • Versus last quarter: The current call placed more emphasis on "higher for longer" rates, intensifying competition (especially CRE), and explicit Q3 net interest income guidance; Q1 focus leaned more on stable deposit costs and a Q2 net interest income range.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance cadence shifted forward: Q1 provided a Q2 net interest income range of $99M-$101M, while Q2 provided a Q3 range of $100M-$102M and reiterated mid-single-digit full-year loan growth. (Executive VP & CFO Bell)
  • Capital return posture stayed active, but Q2 added a dividend increase announcement and discussed buybacks as the primary alternative if M&A is not executed; Q1 emphasized buybacks and noted Durbin impact parameters. (President & Director Paracchini; Executive VP & CFO Bell)
  • Analysts’ questions evolved from Q1’s focus on production levels, deposit-cost trajectory, and securities portfolio growth to Q2’s heavier focus on payoff visibility, second-half expense pressure, and competitive dynamics in deposits and CRE.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management said deposit competition "remains elevated" and loan pricing competition is picking up "particularly in commercial real estate," with the operating stance to "stay disciplined rather than chase volume." (President & Director Paracchini)
  • Payoff volatility was repeatedly flagged as the main uncertainty for reported loan growth, even as originations were described as consistent. (President & Director Paracchini; Executive VP & CFO Bell)
  • Management said it is preparing for crossing $10B in assets, and that preparation is influencing decisions on "growth, expenses and capital." (President & Director Paracchini)

Final Takeaway

Byline’s leadership characterized Q2 as a record-profit quarter backed by higher revenue, controlled expenses, and stable credit trends, while positioning the second half around disciplined relationship growth in a "higher for longer" rate environment. Management’s key forward markers included Q3 net interest income of $100M-$102M, noninterest income of $14M-$15M, continued mid-single-digit full-year loan growth expectations dependent on payoff timing, and an active capital return posture reinforced by a higher quarterly dividend and ongoing buybacks, with M&A framed as selective and metrics-driven.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AmEx forecasts 10% 2026 revenue growth while reinvesting outperformance and holding EPS at $17.30-$17.90
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Express (AXP) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered another excellent quarter with 10% revenue growth and EPS of $4.53" and "based on our better-than-expected performance year-to-date, we are raising our full year revenue growth guidance to 10%" while "we continue to expect full year EPS of $17.30 to $17.90" (Chairman & CEO Stephen Squeri).
  • "If you're outperforming your expectations and you are raising your revenue guidance, why aren't you also raising EPS guidance?" and "we can either drop the overperformance to the bottom line and buy back more shares or we can invest to grow the business further... We've chosen the latter" (Chairman & CEO Squeri).
  • "Our Platinum portfolio is now the fastest growing in our U.S. consumer business" and "65% of new consumer accounts" came from "Millennials and Gen Zs" (Chairman & CEO Squeri).
  • "We had another strong quarter with revenue growth of 10% and EPS up 11% year-over-year" and "provision expense of $1.1 billion included a reserve release of $191 million" (Chief Financial Officer Christophe Le Caillec).
  • "We returned $2.9 billion of capital to our shareholders, including $0.6 billion of dividends and $2.2 billion of share repurchases" and "an ROE of 36% this quarter" (CFO Le Caillec).

Outlook

  • "We are raising our revenue guidance and now expect full year revenue growth of 10%" and "as we increase investments in new customer acquisition and technology development, we are maintaining our full year EPS guidance of $17.30 to $17.90" (CFO Le Caillec).
  • "Starting in Q4, we expect a quarterly impact of around 1 percentage point to spend growth and around 2.5 percentage point impact to net interest income" from the small business co-brand portfolio transfers, and "the impact to revenue -- to total revenue is about 1 percentage point" (CFO Le Caillec).
  • "We expect marketing to be up by around 10% year-over-year in the second half of the year" and "we now expect the ratio to be between 44% and 45% for the full year" for the VCE-to-revenue ratio (CFO Le Caillec).
  • "We expect growth to accelerate in Q3 and to exit the year in the high teens" for card fees (CFO Le Caillec).

Financial Results

  • "We delivered another excellent quarter with 10% revenue growth and EPS of $4.53" (Chairman & CEO Squeri).
  • "Pretax income was up 15%, while net income was up 8% due to prior year tax discretes" and "spend growth stepped up to the highest level we've seen in 3 years" (CFO Le Caillec).
  • "Overall spend was up 9.4% FX adjusted" with "Goods & Services spending up 9% and T&E up 10%" and "global Amex travel bookings up 22% year-over-year" (CFO Le Caillec).
  • "Net card fees reached record levels" and "Net card fees... up 15.4%" while "net interest income was up 11%" (CFO Le Caillec).

Q&A

  • Sanjay Sakhrani, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: "I'm just trying to think about the sustainability of that outperformance?"; CFO Le Caillec: "One of the biggest contributor to the acceleration is the Platinum refresh" and "momentum" is from "new card member acquisition" and "tenured card members that are increasing their spend"; Chairman & CEO Squeri: "with the refresh, engagement has been really accelerated" and cited "a 22% increase in travel bookings".
  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked where reinvestment is going and sustainability into 2027; Chairman & CEO Squeri: investments include "technology" plus "a little bit in TheFork" and "card acquisition," adding "those things weren't on the docket at the beginning of the year" (Agentic Commerce).
  • Donald Fandetti, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked about middle-market and fintech competition; Chairman & CEO Squeri: "it’s middle market where we have seen the softness" and "Center... will help us not only retain business, but actually win new business".
  • Craig Maurer, Financial Technology Partners LP: asked about URR adjustment and incentives; CFO Le Caillec: URR change had "a small benefit" and for the full year "it's going to be de minimis"; Chairman & CEO Squeri: NFL/Fanatics are "in the plan" and "in the run rate".
  • Richard Shane, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked about attrition after fee changes; CFO Le Caillec: "we're not seeing anything on attrition" and retention was "flat year-over-year" after "we raised the fee by $200".
  • Mark DeVries, Deutsche Bank AG, Research Division: asked ROI of Resy/Tock/TheFork; Chairman & CEO Squeri: "we don't look at it" on a stand-alone P&L, calling them "open platforms" that support "customer acquisition" and "card member retention".
  • Terry Ma, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked fee growth acceleration timing; CFO Le Caillec: card fee growth acceleration is driven by Platinum repricing and "we expect that card fees are going to exit this year with a growth rate in the high teens".
  • Robert Wildhack, Autonomous Research US LP: asked NII growth slowdown and outlook; CFO Le Caillec: Q2 included transfer of "the Loews portfolio" impacting NII growth "by about 1 percentage point," and after Q3 transfer "the impact to the NII line is going to be 2.5%" but "impact to net income and earnings is really negligible".
  • Darrin Peller, Wolfe Research, LLC: asked about AI leverage; Chairman & CEO Squeri: in tech, AI is driving "a 30% to 40% decrease in cycle time" and in servicing there was "a lack of acceleration in hiring".
  • Mihir Bhatia, BofA Securities, Research Division: asked July trends and billings assumptions; CFO Le Caillec: "we're not going to get into trying to guide on billing" and expects the momentum to continue, but the portfolio transfers create "about a 1% headwind".

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, with repeated durability questions including "sustainability" (Sakhrani) and probing on "NII growth" noise (Wildhack) and billings visibility (Bhatia).
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and explanatory in Q&A, with Chairman & CEO Squeri emphasizing reinvestment choice ("We've chosen the latter") and CFO Le Caillec repeatedly framing portfolio-sale impacts as "baked in" and "negligible" to earnings.
  • Versus last quarter, management language shifted from reaffirming a range to explicitly raising revenue growth guidance ("raising our full year revenue growth guidance to 10%"), while keeping EPS guidance unchanged and tying it to reinvestment.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language moved from "reaffirming our full year 2026 guidance of 9% to 10% annual revenue growth" (Q1) to "raising our full year revenue growth guidance to 10%" (Q2), while EPS guidance stayed "$17.30 to $17.90" in both quarters (Chairman & CEO Squeri).
  • Q2 emphasized incremental second-half investments and M&A-related needs: "our proposed acquisition of TheFork... did not have it originally in our plan" (Chairman & CEO Squeri), compared with Q1’s heavier focus on launching a U.S. commercial product roadmap and the "ACE Developer Kit".
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from Q1 questions about potential travel disruption ("airline growth softened") to Q2 questions on parsing U.S. consumer strength and NII mechanics tied to the co-brand portfolio transfers.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We do expect to see impacts from the sale of the small business co-brand portfolios" including "around 1 percentage point" spend headwind and "around 2.5 percentage point" NII headwind by Q4 (CFO Le Caillec).
  • Geopolitical/travel disruption was addressed as localized: "there is no evidence of a general slowdown" despite seeing category and route impacts (CFO Le Caillec).
  • Reward-cost variability was flagged: "we have seen stronger spend than we expected" in rewards-heavy categories and "these factors are contributing to a slightly higher VCE ratio than we originally expected" (CFO Le Caillec).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as another quarter of double-digit revenue growth and raised full-year revenue growth guidance to 10% while keeping EPS guidance at $17.30 to $17.90, explicitly attributing the EPS posture to reinvesting outperformance into customer acquisition, technology, and the proposed TheFork acquisition. Executives repeatedly pointed to Platinum-driven engagement, strong credit metrics, and expected second-half headwinds from co-brand portfolio transfers that pressure certain growth rates but were described as having negligible earnings impact.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Anthropic launches Opus 5, closer and cheaper to frontier AI model Fable 5
data.description:
On Friday, Anthropic (ANTHRO) launched its new AI model Claude Opus 5, which comes close to its frontier AI model Claude Fable 5 at half the price.

The AI tech giant noted that Opus 5 is priced the same as Opus 4.8, with materially higher performance across coding, knowledge work, and scientific research benchmarks.

Opus 5 is priced at $5 per million input tokens and $25 per million output tokens, the same as Opus 4.8, which was unveiled in May. Developers can get started with Claude Opus 5 on the Claude API, according to the company.

The new model is also offered in Fast mode, where it runs around 2.5 times the default speed. As with Opus 4.8, Fast mode is available at twice Opus 5's base price on the Claude Platform and through usage credits in Claude Code.

Anthropic noted that Claude Opus 5's safeguards are designed to allow beneficial uses of the model in both cybersecurity and biology. They are similar to those applied to Opus 4.8, except for some stronger guardrails on a narrow range of cyber tasks.

Anthropic is in a tight race with rival OpenAI (OPENAI) to develop advanced AI models. However, managing these models comes with its own set of challenges. U.S. lawmakers have proposed an "AI Kill Switch Act," which would allow federal authorities to halt AI models. This comes just days after OpenAI said its AI agent escaped containment during a security test. ​

Last month, Anthropic was forced to temporarily restrict access to its most advanced AI models Fable 5 and Mythos 5 after the Trump administration issued an export control directive. OpenAI had also staggered the release of GPT-5.6 following a government request.

Opus 5’s cyber classifiers are proportionally less restrictive than those on Fable 5. They allow Opus 5 to find vulnerabilities in source code, but block 'binary-based' vulnerability scanning — a method more likely to be associated with malicious actors — penetration testing, and exploit generation, according to the company.

The company added that since the new model has a similar suite of safeguards to Opus 4.8, it is now its most capable generally available model for scientific research. However, the model still has limitations on long-running, autonomous research tasks. 

Mythos 5 remains the stronger model for some types of biological work, the company noted.

In addition, Opus 5 is much stronger at verifying its work and iterating carefully until it succeeds.

The model also excels on software engineering tasks. On Frontier-Bench v0.1, Opus 5 surpasses all other models and more than doubles Opus 4.8’s performance at a lower cost per task. On CursorBench 3.2, at max effort, the model performs within 0.5% of Fable 5’s peak score, but at half the cost per task, Anthropic added.

data.title: Amerant outlines path to $9.1B deposits and $7.3B loans by Q4 2026 while targeting ~1% ROA by year-end
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Amerant Bancorp (AMTB) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Our objective is clear: stabilize the business, strengthen the foundation and positioning the company for disciplined, sustainable growth and improved shareholder value," said Carlos Iafigliola (President, CEO & Director), outlining four initiatives: "transforming credit," "operational efficiency," "relationship first," and "grow the bank."

  • "On the deposit side, momentum remains strong with total deposits increasing over $400 million, primarily driven by international deposit growth," said President, CEO & Director Iafigliola, adding: "Venezuelan deposits increasing close to $500 million from the first quarter" and describing them as "operating deposits tied to essential industries" that are "supported by our existing compliance, due diligence, and relationship management framework."

  • "Diluted earnings per share for the second quarter was $0.53 compared to $0.44 in the first quarter," said Sharymar Yepez (Senior EVP & CFO), while also reporting: "Net interest income was $82.6 million" and "Noninterest expense was $68.9 million."

  • "Our CET1 remains strong at 11.94% compared to 11.84% last quarter," said Senior EVP & CFO Yepez, adding: "we repurchased 690,000 shares at a weighted average price of $23.29 per share" and "Our Board of Directors just approved a quarterly dividend of $0.09 per share payable on August 28 of this year."

Outlook

  • "We expect total loans to reach approximately $7.3 billion by the fourth quarter of 2026" and "we expect total deposits to reach approximately $9.1 billion by the fourth quarter of 2026," said Senior EVP & CFO Yepez.

  • "We expect net interest margin to be approximately 3.50% for the remainder of the year," said Senior EVP & CFO Yepez, and in Q&A added: "No rate hikes, no drops either that would cause anything significant, at least through December of this year."

  • "From an expense perspective, we are projecting 3Q expenses to be in line with 2Q, declining to a range of $66 million to $67 million in 4Q '26" and "make progress toward a target efficiency ratio of approximately 60%," said Senior EVP & CFO Yepez; she also guided to a profitability milestone: "we expect to reach 1% by year-end."

  • Compared with Q1 guidance language, management’s NIM framing shifted from "stabilizing around 3.4% towards year-end" (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez, Q1) to "approximately 3.50% for the remainder of the year" (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez, Q2).

Financial Results

  • "Total assets were $10.3 billion" at quarter end, "total deposits were $8.4 billion" and "total gross loans were $6.9 billion," said Senior EVP & CFO Yepez.

  • "Net interest margin was 3.52% compared to 3.55% in the first quarter," said Senior EVP & CFO Yepez, attributing the change to "lower loan yields" that were "largely offset by a lower cost of funds and continued growth in lower-cost international deposits."

  • Credit costs and asset quality metrics improved sequentially as reported: "Provision for credit losses was $4.8 million compared to $7.8 million in the first quarter" and "Nonperforming loans were down $5 million or 2.8% to $171 million or 1.7% of total assets," said Senior EVP & CFO Yepez; she also noted: "Subsequent to quarter end, a $9 million New York CRE loan was paid off, bringing NPLs further down to $162 million."

  • Expense and capital-return items included one-time and mix effects: "Noninterest expense this quarter includes" higher variable compensation, "the last portion of a sports partnership agreement that was terminated," and "the absence of $1.7 million in investment impairment expense" from the prior quarter, said Senior EVP & CFO Yepez.

Q&A

  • Woody Lay, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: "I was just curious how much of that incremental deposit growth will be used for funding remix"; President, CEO & Director Iafigliola: "we have let go hypersensitive deposits" and "Most of these new deposits coming our way are 0 cost, noninterest-bearing"; Senior EVP & CFO Yepez: "we do expect to use a significant portion being redeployed into high-quality assets" and "pay off maturing wholesale funds."

  • Woody Lay, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: "Do you expect to be over the $10 billion in asset threshold by year-end '26?"; President, CEO & Director Iafigliola: "Yes. Yes, we do expect to."

  • Woody Lay, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: "some of the moving pieces on how the margin stays flat"; President, CEO & Director Iafigliola: "we are operating in markets that are very competitive" and "the diminishing yield on the new asset generation will definitely make us feel like 3.50-ish is the right financial margin for the rest of the year."

  • Woody Lay, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: "are they related to any specific industry?"; President, CEO & Director Iafigliola: "they are specifically tied to the oil production" and "most of the funds are related to institutional depository base."

  • Russell Elliott Gunther, Stephens Inc., Research Division: "any update into changes in loan size targets or asset classes"; President, CEO & Director Iafigliola: "the new sweet spot for Amerant transactions typically close to the $30 million" and "typically, what we are fostering is granularity across the portfolio"; Senior EVP & CFO Yepez: "expectation overall would be C&I space."

  • Russell Elliott Gunther, Stephens Inc., Research Division: "are you assuming anything out of the Fed"; Senior EVP & CFO Yepez: "No rate hikes, no drops either" through December.

  • Russell Elliott Gunther, Stephens Inc., Research Division: crossing $10B and expense implications; President, CEO & Director Iafigliola: "most of the investments that were related to crossing the $10 billion we're already factoring into our run rate"; Senior EVP & CFO Yepez: "the expenses or the investments... are embedded into the figures that we are providing from a guidance standpoint."

  • Cole Martin, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: "talk a bit more about your NIM and NII expectations" for the "1% ROA target"; Senior EVP & CFO Yepez: "having access and having inflows from low-cost deposits and being able to redeploy them into high-quality assets provide the necessary contribution to NII to get to that 1% ROA."

  • Cole Martin, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: balancing loan growth and repurchases; President, CEO & Director Iafigliola: "Our CET1 close to 12%" and "we will continue to use our buyback" and "we have approximately $6 million left."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive but probing on deposit sustainability, margin, and regulatory/scale effects, including: "it was really encouraging" (Woody Lay, KBW) alongside detailed follow-ups on NIM and Venezuela concentration.

  • Management tone was slightly positive and execution-focused, leaning on specificity and confidence statements such as: "Yes. Yes, we do expect to" cross $10B in assets (President, CEO & Director Iafigliola) and "This gives us greater confidence in our path" on efficiency (President, CEO & Director Iafigliola).

  • Versus Q1, management’s tone remained confident on strategy execution, while the framing around Venezuela deposits moved from opportunity-building (Q1: "Venezuela represents a key opportunity") to materially larger realized inflows (Q2: "Venezan deposits increasing close to $500 million from the first quarter").

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Leadership role changed between calls, with Carlos Iafigliola introduced in Q1 as "Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director" and in Q2 as "President, CEO & Director."

  • Strategic structure expanded from Q1’s "3 priorities" (stabilizing, credit optimization, sustainable growth) to Q2’s "4 strategic initiatives" including explicit emphasis on "relationship first" and "grow the bank" (President, CEO & Director Iafigliola).

  • Guidance emphasis shifted from Q1’s quarterly expense stabilization "around $68 million" in the back half of 2026 (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez, Q1) to a steeper Q4 run-rate reduction "to a range of $66 million to $67 million" and a defined profitability bridge to "reach 1%" ROA by year-end (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez, Q2).

Risks and Concerns

  • Margin pressure from competitive pricing was highlighted in Q&A: "we are operating in markets that are very competitive" and "the high-quality assets... tend to be more tighter in nature," said President, CEO & Director Iafigliola.

  • Deposit concentration and compliance management around Venezuela growth was addressed with mitigation language: "we will continue to manage concentration, compliance and pricing discipline carefully," said President, CEO & Director Iafigliola.

  • Commercial real estate remained a managed area as management described portfolio cleanup dynamics: "some of the classified that we still have and some of the NPLs that we still have are in the CRE portfolio," said President, CEO & Director Iafigliola.

Final Takeaway

Management positioned Q2 as a step forward in its reset, tying stronger international deposit inflows—particularly Venezuela—to funding remix, liquidity, and a path to higher profitability. Executives guided to approximately $9.1 billion in deposits and approximately $7.3 billion in loans by Q4 2026, modeled NIM at approximately 3.50% for the rest of the year, and projected Q4 expenses of $66 million to $67 million while working toward an approximately 60% efficiency ratio and a 1% ROA by year-end, alongside continued dividends and share repurchases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Orchid Island Capital signals leverage management as book value was down 4.3% as of last night
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Orchid Island Capital (ORC) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Robert Cauley (Chairman, President & CEO) framed Q2 positioning around a shifting macro backdrop, citing “a change at the head of the Fed” and describing new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh as “very, very strongly against inflation,” adding Warsh said inflation above target for 5 years “is unacceptable.” Cauley said these forces “tend to flatten the curve,” and added war-related uncertainty could keep driving market volatility.

  • Jerry Sintes (VP & Treasurer) reported Q2 results: “During Q2, we earned $0.44 per share,” with book value “$7.22 compared to $7.08 at the start of the quarter,” and “Total return during the quarter was 6.2%.” Sintes also said the company reduced the dividend: “our dividend during Q2 was $0.30, which we reduced from $0.36 during Q1.”

  • Robert Cauley (Chairman, President & CEO) said portfolio changes were incremental, emphasizing “not a lot changed” and that the firm “increased our share count by about 1.5%.” He said the portfolio is “now all 30 years” and concentrated near par (5s, 5.5s, 6s), with a shift “slightly down in coupon” because “specified pool performance has not been that great of late.”

Outlook

  • The company did not provide formal EPS or revenue guidance in the prepared remarks or Q&A.

  • Robert Cauley (Chairman, President & CEO) said the company may need to adjust positioning as the portfolio extends: “we are likely to have to start making some slight changes to the portfolio just to account for the fact that our portfolio is extending.” He also said leverage had drifted higher after quarter-end: “As of last night, it’s up to about 7.73. So leverage has extended as book value has moved and mortgages have extended.”

  • Cauley tied dividend sustainability to portfolio earnings power, stating: “if I take our average book value for Q2… get a yield of about 16.8%. And then if I look at what we were earning on the portfolio using GAAP measures, we’re right around the same level, right around 16.7%.”

Financial Results

  • Jerry Sintes (VP & Treasurer) said Q2 prepayments slowed and leverage declined: “prepayment speeds of 10.9% compared to 14.7% in Q1,” and “Economic leverage ratio at the end of Q2 was 7.3:1 compared to 7.9:1 at the end of Q1.”

  • Robert Cauley (Chairman, President & CEO) described spread and carry pressures within the quarter, citing “a 1 basis point decline in the yield of the portfolio from 5.75% to 5.74%, and a 5 basis point increase in our economic funding costs,” which he said resulted in “the 6 basis point decline in our net interest spread.”

  • Cauley highlighted hedge repositioning: “we added some 5-year and 10-year swap positions,” and said hedge coverage increased, with repo funding covered by hedges rising “from 72% at the end of Q1 to 91% at the end of Q2,” while swap notional balance increased “from about $7.9 billion to $10.1 billion.”

Q&A

  • Douglas Harter, BTIG, LLC, Research Division: Asked how economic funding costs may trend if hikes occur and how GAAP versus dividend framing differs; Robert Cauley (Chairman, President & CEO) answered that with “about… 91%” hedge coverage, he would expect costs “to remain fairly stable,” but noted “that’s not 100%, so there would be some leakage into our funding costs,” and said dividend impact “depends on what happens on the asset side.”

  • Douglas Harter, BTIG, LLC, Research Division: Asked about incremental returns versus the dividend-required return; Cauley said “They’re starting to move higher,” linked uncertainty to the war, and added, “I don’t expect that we’re going to be able to raise capital,” citing the stock “trading well below book.”

  • Jason Weaver, JonesTrading Institutional Services, LLC, Research Division: Asked where the most attractive coupons/cohorts are for deployment and what ROEs look like; Cauley said ROEs were “somewhere in the 16% to 17% range” and could be “moving higher,” and flagged “call it 5% to 6.5%” as a likely attractive range as “the dust hopefully settles.”

  • Jason Stewart, Compass Point Research & Trading, LLC, Research Division: Asked how hedge ratios (swaps vs. broader coverage) flow through to the dividend in a flattening curve; Cauley said, “the curve is going to flatten… and the fact that only 70% of the book is in swaps… is kind of locked in,” but added he did not expect “dramatic decreases in the dividend” because he expected mortgages to “cheapen some more” rather than spreads compressing materially.

  • Jason Stewart, Compass Point Research & Trading, LLC, Research Division: Asked if the company would hold the dividend level through an interim ROE dip; Cauley responded, “I don’t know if they’d be willing to do that,” and said he did not expect “wholesale changes to the portfolio.”

  • Mikhail Goberman, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC, Research Division: Asked about the 2% expense ratio and the year-over-year increase in expenses; Cauley said “Management and staff were rewarded with bonuses this year,” specified “The awards are all 100% in shares, stock, no cash,” and called it “a one-off award,” adding, “I would expect to see this line continue to trend down.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly cautious, concentrating on dividend durability under rate hikes, incremental ROEs during volatility, and expense discipline, including pointed framing on “passive rates and the dividend” and whether the company would “hold the dividend level” through an interim reset.

  • Management tone was slightly negative on near-term uncertainty but operationally steady, repeatedly tying outcomes to war-driven volatility and policy uncertainty, including “there’s quite a bit of uncertainty with respect to the war” and “markets don’t like uncertainty,” while also stating the portfolio yield remained aligned with the dividend.

  • Compared with Q1’s prepared-remark optimism that “everything is in a very good state… very conducive… and leaves us very bullish,” Q2 commentary placed more weight on post-quarter market dislocation and book value pressure, with Cauley updating book value as “down 4.3%” as of last night (including dividend accrual).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q2 marked a shift from capital deployment emphasis in Q1 to limited growth, with Cauley saying in Q2, “We’re not active in raising new capital,” versus Q1’s description of meaningful portfolio growth and deployment.

  • Dividend policy moved lower in Q2 (to $0.30) after Q1’s $0.36 level, while management in Q2 explicitly linked dividend yield to GAAP portfolio earnings (16.8% vs. 16.7%).

  • Hedging posture intensified in Q2, increasing repo hedge coverage to 91% and adding 5-year and 10-year swaps, compared with Q1’s framework described as “approximately 65% of our repo balance” with an emphasis on swaps.

  • Tone evolved from Q1’s discussion of stable rates and improving conditions toward Q2’s discussion of a new Fed chair, tighter swap spreads, and war-driven uncertainty affecting rates, volatility, and near-term book value.

Risks and Concerns

  • Robert Cauley (Chairman, President & CEO) identified war-driven uncertainty as a central risk: “there’s quite a bit of uncertainty with respect to the war, how that’s going to impact rates, the economy and what the Fed is going to do.”

  • Cauley highlighted swap spread moves as a hedge-performance risk: “swap spreads have been moving more negative,” adding, “That affects the performance of swaps as hedges.”

  • Funding conditions were described as drifting less favorable from unusually strong levels: “we are starting to see some movement away from this very, very attractive levels,” though Cauley added, “there’s no reason for us to think that there’s anything ominous on the horizon.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as a quarter of improved reported results and a reduced dividend alongside incremental portfolio and hedge adjustments, while emphasizing that recent post-quarter market moves—driven by war uncertainty, a more hawkish Fed leadership transition, and tighter swap spreads—have pressured book value and increased leverage, prompting plans for modest positioning changes as the portfolio extends and as mortgage valuations potentially become more attractive for future capital deployment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Safety Insurance soars 41% on $1.54B Mapfre buyout deal
data.description:
Shares of Safety Insurance Group (SAFT), a regional property and casualty insurer, surged 41% on Friday after the company announced it had agreed to be acquired by an affiliate of Mapfre in an all-cash deal valued at approximately $1.54 billion. The acquisition, announced after the previous session's close, will deliver $105 per share to shareholders, a 44% premium to the prior closing price of $72.94.

Under the merger deal, Safety Insurance, which operates in Massachusetts, New Hampshire, and Maine, will continue to do business under its existing brand as a wholly owned Mapfre U.S.A. subsidiary. The deal is expected to close in Q1 2027, subject to regulatory approval.

What Happened to Safety Insurance Group

Trading volume surged to 949,508 shares on Friday, 7.62 times the stock's average daily volume, as investors responded to the buyout announcement. Shares opened at $103.07 and traded in a narrow range between $102.94 and $103.39, reflecting the market's rapid repricing toward the $105 per-share deal price.

Prior to today's move, SAFT had drifted lower across much of the preceding two weeks. The stock fell in four of the five sessions before July 23, losing ground in a pattern of modest but persistent declines before the acquisition news reset the stock's entire valuation framework overnight.

By the Numbers: Safety Insurance Group

  • Today's price: $103.06, up 41.29% from the prior close of $72.94
  • Deal price: $105.00 per share in cash
  • Deal premium: 44% to the prior closing price
  • Deal value: Approximately $1.54 billion
  • Volume: 949,508 shares traded — 7.62x average daily volume
  • 50-day moving average: $72.66
  • 100-day moving average: $73.42
  • 200-day moving average: $74.43
  • RSI (14-day): 45.07 — a neutral reading reflecting range-bound behavior prior to the acquisition announcement

Latest Analyst Commentary on Safety Insurance Group

Following the merger announcement, Seeking Alpha analyst Seeking Profits characterized the Mapfre transaction as a bet on the benefits of larger scale. The analyst noted that the 1.8x book value acquisition price represents a "full standalone price" that makes competing bids unlikely.

The analysis highlighted SAFT's roughly 1.75% upside to the $105 deal price and an approximately 7% annualized return over an assumed six-to-seven month closing window, alongside a 3.6% dividend yield expected to continue through closing. Read more

data.title: GrafTech outlines 5%-10% 2026 sales volume growth while advancing >15% higher price commitments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: GrafTech International (EAF) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “We delivered strong sales volume growth, increased production and capacity utilization, and further improved our manufacturing cost structure,” and “reaffirmed our full year sales volume and cost expectations” (CEO, President & Director Timothy Flanagan).

  • “Since announcing these pricing actions near the end of the first quarter, we have secured customer commitments at prices that are on average more than 15% above those achieved prior to the announcement,” while “more than 90% of our anticipated volume” is “already committed” and “this will not translate immediately into higher realized pricing” (CEO Flanagan).

  • “Commerce is expected to announce its preliminary countervailing duty determination early next week,” and “we expect Commerce will announce its preliminary determination on anti-dumping duties by the end of September” (CEO Flanagan).

  • “Sales volume increased to approximately 31,000 metric tons,” “net sales for the quarter were $127 million,” and “adjusted EBITDA was $2 million” (CFO & Senior VP Rory O'Donnell).

Outlook

  • Management repeated its volume and cost framework: “our second quarter and year-to-date performance is consistent with our expectation for full year sales volume growth of between 5% and 10%,” and “we continue to reconfirm our guidance of a modest year-over-year reduction in our cash COGS” (CFO O'Donnell; CEO Flanagan).

  • Cost guidance language emphasized pass-through: “sustained increases in key input costs will need to be reflected in graphite electrode pricing, beyond the pricing actions we have already announced” (CFO O'Donnell), and “there should be no expectation in the market that we're going to bear that inflationary impact” (CEO Flanagan).

  • Compared with Q1, management kept the same full-year sales volume growth range and reiterated that pricing uplift is expected to “begin contributing to higher realized pricing” over “future quarters,” with a stronger emphasis that “improved pricing has the potential to contribute meaningfully to our financial performance beginning in 2027” (CFO O'Donnell).

Financial Results

  • Q2 revenue was $127 million vs. $125 million (analysts’ revenue estimate for Q2 2026).

  • Operational metrics and pricing were framed around mix and lagged realization: “production volume exceeded 33,000 metric tons,” “capacity utilization of 74%,” and “weighted average realized pricing was approximately $3,900 per metric ton” (CFO O'Donnell).

  • Cash flow and working-capital seasonality were highlighted: “net cash used in operating activities during the second quarter was $69 million,” and “we expect operating cash flow to improve during the second half of the year as inventory levels normalize” (CFO O'Donnell).

  • Liquidity and financing actions were a focal update: “we drew the remaining $100 million available under our delayed draw first lien term loan,” ending with “approximately $253 million of total liquidity,” and “we filed a shelf registration” and “established an at-the-market equity program” where “usage has been modest to date” (CFO O'Donnell).

Q&A

  • Bennett Moore, JPMorgan Chase & Co: Asked if U.S. volumes were pulled forward and whether strength persists; CEO Flanagan said “we are seeing some pull into the second quarter,” and “we expect kind of the back half of the year to continue with strength in the U.S.”

  • Bennett Moore, JPMorgan Chase & Co: Asked about needle coke and decant oil inflation; CFO O'Donnell said “$200 to $300 price increases” are being seen, adding “there has been some inbounds received to determine whether or not there's availability of our supply in Texas.”

  • Arun Viswanathan, RBC Capital Markets: Pressed on utilization and whether capacity rationalization is needed; CEO Flanagan said electrodes remain “a market that is oversupplied” and “if the market dictates that supply needs to come out of the market… we'll adjust our production accordingly.”

  • Arun Viswanathan, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about pricing path amid oversupply; CEO Flanagan said the Q1 action was “the first step” with “momentum going into negotiations in the fourth quarter,” and pointed to “a decline of about 10% in Chinese exports.”

  • Kirk Ludtke, Raymond James: Asked about energy inflation, lag, and offset; CFO O'Donnell said “we're still sticking with $3,600 to $3,700 a ton,” and noted “almost 70%” of EU back-half needs are under fixed price contracts; CEO Flanagan added inflation “will be passed through to customers through pricing.”

  • Bennett Moore, JPMorgan Chase & Co: Asked about 2027 hedging direction; CFO O'Donnell said the company is “aiming for similar protections,” and “it's fair to assume” next-year levels are “directionally” higher.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: Slightly negative/pressing, with repeated probing on sustainability of U.S. demand, inflation magnitude, hedging, oversupply, and the need for “rationalization of capacity” (Arun Viswanathan, RBC Capital Markets).

  • Management: Slightly positive in prepared remarks (“We delivered strong sales volume growth”), more measured in Q&A on oversupply and input costs, repeatedly leaning on control levers and pass-through (“we'll adjust our production accordingly”; “will be passed through to customers”) (CEO Flanagan).

  • Versus Q1, the tone moved from early “signs of improvement” and “macro uncertainty” to stronger execution emphasis (“meaningful progress”) and more concrete financing/tooling updates (CEO Flanagan; CFO O'Donnell).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Pricing actions progressed from announcing a “minimum of $600 to $1,200 per metric ton” increase in Q1 to reporting Q2 commitments “on average more than 15% above” pre-announcement levels, while reiterating the realization lag into later shipments (CEO Flanagan).

  • Liquidity messaging shifted from expected future borrowing to completed action: Q1 said the company would “most likely” draw the delayed draw term loan by end of Q2; Q2 confirmed it “drew the remaining $100 million” and added a shelf registration and ATM program (CFO O'Donnell).

  • Analysts’ focus remained consistent (pricing recovery, input inflation, trade cases), while Q2 added deeper questioning on hedging into 2027 and the practical mechanics of supply rationalization (analysts; CEO Flanagan; CFO O'Donnell).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing external pressures: “geopolitical conflicts” driving “higher oil-related feedstock costs” and “potential disruptions in decant oil availability,” alongside continued “pricing pressure across much of the graphite electrode industry” (CEO Flanagan; CFO O'Donnell).

  • Mitigations emphasized vertical integration and contracting: “our captive supply of needle coke down in Port Lavaca, Texas” and EU power coverage with “fixed price contracts” (CFO O'Donnell).

Final Takeaway

GrafTech management framed Q2 as execution progress amid still-depressed industry pricing, pointing to higher volumes, the highest utilization since 2022, and a meaningful cash-cost improvement cadence while reaffirming full-year volume growth and cash COGS expectations. Management emphasized that recently secured customer commitments averaging more than 15% above pre-announcement levels and upcoming U.S. trade determinations are intended to rebuild pricing power over time, while liquidity actions—drawing the remaining $100 million term loan capacity and adding shelf/ATM optionality—were positioned as tools to navigate the cycle and support a pricing and earnings inflection “beginning in 2027.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
